Series Bible · Version 2.0

Avery Ivey

WILDFLOWER

8-Episode Limited Series · Netflix Original · Drama / Music

Coming Into Her Own

TV-MA  ·  45–55 min  ·  Drama / Music / Character Study  ·  Confidential — Industry Use Only

Read

01 — Logline & Premise

When a breakout country anthem launches a small-town singer into overnight fame, she must navigate industry pressure, a complicated almost-romance with her producer, and a public downfall — before finding her identity somewhere between who she was and who the world wanted her to be.

Wildflower is an 8-episode limited series about the cost of becoming someone the world decides you should be — and the harder, quieter work of becoming yourself instead.

It is a music show that happens to be a character study. It is a coming-of-age story that refuses to let its protagonist stay a girl. It is a love story where the most important relationship is the one Avery Ivey has with her own voice — literally and figuratively.

Set against the Nashville country music industry, Wildflower tracks Avery from county fair unknown to platinum-selling artist to broken-down almost-nobody, and finally to something she never had a word for before: herself.

If the version of you the world loves was never really you — what do you do with everything they gave her?
Format
8-Episode Limited Series
Platform
Netflix Original
Episode Length
45–55 Minutes
Rating
TV-MA
Time Span
~3 Years
Music Catalog
34 Original Songs
Setting
Small-Town Texas → Nashville → Touring → Home

02 — Four-Arc Structure

The series maps eight episodes to four emotional registers. Two per arc. The show shifts as Avery's world shifts.

Arc One · Episodes 1–2
The Bloom
Ep 01  ·  Roots   |   Ep 02  ·  The Bloom
Wonder / Innocence / Momentum
Anchor Song: "Wildflower"
Palette: Ivory Cream · Burnt Sienna · Golden Hour Amber
Arc Two · Episodes 3–4
The Illusion
Ep 03  ·  Brand Avery   |   Ep 04  ·  Almost Loved You
Seduction / Erosion / Denial
Anchor Song: "Almost Loved You"
Palette: Deep Teal · Studio Blue · Spotlight White
Arc Three · Episodes 5–6
The Long Way Down
Ep 05  ·  Loud Enough   |   Ep 06  ·  The Floor
Collapse / Grief / Rock Bottom
Anchor Song: "The Long Way Down"
Palette: Near-Monochrome · Grey-Green · Stark Fluorescent
Arc Four · Episodes 7–8
Somewhere in the Middle
Ep 07  ·  Somewhere in the Middle   |   Ep 08  ·  Hard Truths
Rebuild / Honesty / Homecoming
Anchor Song: "Hard Truths"
Palette: Warm Neutrals · Natural Sage · Morning Gold

03 — Episode Guide

Eight episodes. Four arcs. One unraveling — and what comes after.

01
The Bloom · Part 1
Roots
"She was always going to be someone. She just didn't know who yet."
Read More ↓

Episode 1 is the before. Unhurried. It breathes. We open on the county fair stage — acoustic guitar, a small crowd, 'Wildflower' performed raw. Then the episode doubles back: the house, Carol in the kitchen, Al on the porch, the bedroom wall of notebooks. The specific texture of a Texas town that shaped her without her knowing it. The viral moment lands at the very end. It's not triumphant. It's terrifying.

Music
"Wildflower" — Opening performance · full, acoustic, completely unpolished
"Tonight It Belongs to Me" — Car radio driving home from the fair
"Back to Chapter One" — End-of-episode underscore as the numbers climb
02
The Bloom · Part 2
The Bloom
"She thought she made it. She was right. That was the problem."
Read More ↓

The machine arrives. The label. The manager. The stylist brief. We watch the first reshaping happen — not maliciously, but irresistibly. Steve Powers appears. He doesn't try to change her. He asks what the song is actually about. Nobody's asked that yet. She notices. Doesn't say anything about it.

Music
"Dreams I Packed Away" — Packing-for-Nashville montage
"Tonight It Belongs to Me" — Nashville showcase performance
"Brand New Me" — Nashville arrival montage · the construction of Brand Avery
03
The Illusion · Part 1
Brand Avery
"She was performing okayness so well she started to believe it."
Read More ↓

The tour is running. The numbers are good. She is doing every interview correctly. In the studio with Steve, the work is the best she's made. The conversation spills past the music. He's grounded in a way she's forgotten she wanted to be. The air between them starts to feel like something. She doesn't name it.

Music
"Lookin' Okay" — Press interview underscore · the irony is intentional
"Sunday Morning Coffee" — Studio session warmth · the almost
"People Pleaser" — Tour montage · performing a version of herself
04
The Illusion · Part 2
Almost Loved You
"He said it gently, like he knew what he was doing to the room."
Read More ↓

Steve tells her he's getting engaged. He says it gently, like he knows what he's doing to the room. She says congratulations. She means it and doesn't mean it simultaneously. She goes back to the hotel and writes "Almost Loved You" instead of sleeping. The midpoint: a panic attack mid-interview, spun as exhaustion. The machine keeps moving. She keeps moving with it. But she felt the gap.

Music
"Always the Almost" — End-of-episode underscore · the country version
"I Still Save It" — Tour bus scene · private grief, public performance
"Almost Loved You" — Label showcase performance · he's in the front row
05
The Long Way Down · Part 1
Loud Enough
"The room didn't know what to do with it. She played it anyway."
Read More ↓

Burnout accumulates. A creative blowup with the label. A leaked recording. She performs "Loud Enough" at a live show to lukewarm reception — too rock for the country crowd. She walks off mid-show. Not dramatic. Exhausted. Sets the mic on the stand and walks into the wing. Then the call comes. Her dad is gone. She walks out of a management meeting mid-sentence.

Music
"Loud Enough" — Live show · wrong crowd, wrong moment · plays in full
"Two Cups" — Pre-breakdown stillness · the last quiet moment
"STATIC" — Panic attack sequence · Episode 5
06
The Long Way Down · Part 2
The Floor
"The grief went somewhere deeper. She carried it back to Nashville like weight."
Read More ↓

Al's funeral is small, rural, completely at odds with everything Avery's life has become. She can't cry yet. Back in Nashville, she becomes more distant and blank. Isolation deepens. The most devastating moments are the smallest: a meal she doesn't eat. A call she answers with nothing to say. A song she starts playing and stops after four bars.

Music
"A Soft Place to Fall" — Al Ivey montage · the most direct emotional statement the show makes
"The Long Way Down" — Full version over the collapse montage · sparse, devastating
"Text with God" — Funeral scene underscore · sparse, searching
07
Somewhere in the Middle · Part 1
Somewhere in the Middle
"She didn't go back to who she was. She went forward to who she'd been avoiding."
Read More ↓

The rebuild isn't a montage. It's slow and uncomfortable and honest. Avery goes home — not as a triumph, just as a direction. Carol at the door. The specific silence of a parent who knows not to ask yet. Six weeks of not writing — shown without comment. When the writing comes back, it comes back different. She calls a small venue in her hometown. They have a Friday open. She books it herself.

Music
"Somewhere in the Middle" — The hopeful turn · plays over the coming-home sequence
"Backroads After Rain" — Texas driving · back in her element · no destination required
"The Long Way Home" — End of episode · she's not there yet, but she knows the direction
08
Somewhere in the Middle · Part 2
Hard Truths
"Not screaming fans. Just connection. It turned out that was the whole point."
Read More ↓

The finale earns its ending by not overreaching for one. No label reconciliation. No reunion with Steve. No industry redemption. Just Avery, a small stage, 300 seats, and 'Wildflower' played the way she actually hears it now — slower, more honest, with the weight of everything that's happened to it since the county fair. Steve sends a text during the show. She reads it in the wings. Puts her phone away. The show doesn't reveal what it said.

Music
"People Pleaser" — Pre-show underscore · she's done performing a version of herself
"Wildflower (Acoustic)" — Closing performance · slower, honest, stripped of everything that wasn't hers
"Hard Truths" — End credits · full version · the songwriter's confession

04 — Characters

Seven people. One story.

Series Lead
Avery Ivey
Mid-20s · Small-town Texas · Singer-Songwriter
She grew up feeling like the wrong kind of girl for the life she was handed — not rebellious, just never quite the shape the world expected. She carries the low hum of a lifetime of not quite fitting, and that tension is what made 'Wildflower' true. Her central flaw is that she's too good at performing okayness. Her central strength is her songwriter's instinct — she processes the world through language and melody.
"She doesn't lose herself because the industry breaks her. She loses herself because she was never sure exactly who she was to begin with. Finding out costs everything."
The Producer
Steve Powers
Early 30s · Nashville · The Almost
Calm, perceptive, and genuinely talented. He hears Avery's music the way she meant it — which almost nobody else in Nashville does. He's not a villain. He's not careless. He's a man with a fiancée, a career, and a moral line he chooses not to cross — and he holds that line even while knowing exactly what it costs her. He is the almost: almost-mentor, almost-love interest, almost-the-one-person-who-really-saw-her.
Steve's Fiancée
Bailey
Late 20s · Warm · Grounded
Warm, grounded, completely at ease in the world Avery is still figuring out how to navigate. She's not a villain either, which is important. She's simply a person who loves Steve and has no idea she's standing in the middle of something complicated. The show treats her with full humanity. She never finds out. She doesn't need to. Her function in the story is structural: she makes the almost permanent.
A&R Representative
Lori Howard
Late 30s · Sharp · Strategic
Sharp, strategic, and genuinely good at her job. She signed Avery, which means she believes in the music — and also means she has a stake in making sure it earns. She is the face of the machine that reshapes Avery, and she does it with warmth and conviction. That's what makes her complicated. She doesn't think of herself as the antagonist. She thinks of herself as the person who makes artists successful.
Head of the Label
Brent Calloway
Early 50s · Pragmatic · The Ceiling
Affable, decisive, and completely fluent in the language of return on investment. He isn't cruel — he's pragmatic in a way that functions like cruelty when applied to a person. He barely thinks about Avery as a person. He thinks about the asset, the brand, the quarter. He's the structural ceiling. He appears in Episodes 2, 4, and 5. Each appearance is shorter than the last. That's intentional.
Avery's Father
Al Ivey
Late 50s · Quiet Believer · Dies Episode 5
A quiet man who believed in his daughter completely and said so in the way quiet men do — not often, but unmistakably. He dies offscreen in Episode 5 — a phone call, mid-management meeting. The funeral is small, rural, completely at odds with everything her life has become. She can't cry yet. His song is 'A Soft Place to Fall.' It plays over the montage of what he meant to her.
Avery's Mother
Carol Ivey
Mid-50s · The Anchor · Episodes 1, 6, 7, 8
The woman who always supported Avery's music without ever being sure it was going to be okay — and who was right on both counts. Her presence marks the before, the bottom, and the return. She doesn't push. She doesn't have dramatic speeches. She has food on the table and a bedroom that still looks the same. In Episode 7, when Avery comes home and Carol opens the door and says nothing for a beat — that's the most important scene in the arc.

06 — Visual Identity

The cinematography tells you where Avery is before she does.

The Bloom
Episodes 1–2
Warm golden hour. Natural light. Slightly soft. The world as something worth keeping. This is Avery before the machine. Every frame has the quality of something you'd want to hold onto.
Ivory Cream
Burnt Sienna
Golden Hour Amber
The Illusion
Episodes 3–4
High contrast. Artificial light. Glass and reflection everywhere. Multiple versions of Avery in every frame. The world is brighter but something is wrong with it. The seams are starting to show.
Deep Teal
Studio Blue
Spotlight White
The Long Way Down
Episodes 5–6
Desaturated. Hard shadows. Wide shots that make her small in frame. Empty spaces. Sound design carries as much weight as image. The colour drains out of her world as she drains out of herself.
Near-Monochrome
Grey-Green
Stark Fluorescent
Somewhere in the Middle
Episodes 7–8
Natural light returns — but morning light, not golden hour. Cooler. Clearer. The warmth is earned now, not given. This is the version of beauty that comes after surviving something.
Warm Neutrals
Natural Sage
Morning Gold

Tonal References

A Complete Unknown (2024)
Daisy Jones & The Six
Normal People
I, Tonya

07 — The Pitch

Why This. Why Now.

The entire original soundtrack already exists. Every song placed in this bible has been written, recorded, and produced. This is not a pitch for a show about music. It is a pitch for a show that already has its music.

The music limited series is having a genuine moment. A Complete Unknown. Daisy Jones. The audience for music-first character drama is established and hungry. Wildflower sits squarely in that lane with one additional asset none of those projects had: 34 original songs, all recorded, all placed, all ready.

That changes the math considerably. It eliminates one of the largest creative risks in music-driven narratives — whether the songs will be good enough to carry the emotional weight the story asks of them. They will. They already do.

The show has a specific and underserved point of view. Country music-adjacent drama tends to go one of two directions — romanticized nostalgia or cynical industry expose. Wildflower is neither. It's a character study that uses the country world as pressure, not subject. Avery's story works in any high-pressure creative industry. It works best here because the music is specific, the world is specific, and the Texas roots are real.

The Audience

Primary
Women 24–38 who watch Normal People, Fleabag, Daisy Jones. They want emotional precision, not emotional manipulation. They know the difference.
Secondary
Country and Red Dirt music listeners who want to see their world treated with intelligence rather than cliché.
Tertiary
Anyone who has ever felt like the version of them the world wants is crowding out the version that's real. Which is most people, eventually.

A conversation. Full episode treatments, expanded scene breakdowns, and the complete original soundtrack catalog are available on request. This project is ready to develop.
